course_info.title: Cognitive Psychology for Designers
prerequisites: UX101 - Introduction to User Experience Design

class_schedule.topic: Perception and Attention in Interface Design
learning_objectives: Apply Gestalt principles of perception to visual interface design
Understand selective and divided attention and their implications for UI complexity
Design interfaces that work with, not against, human perceptual limitations
readings: 100 Things Every Designer Needs to Know About People by Susan Weinschenk, Ch. 1-10
Thinking, Fast and Slow by Daniel Kahneman, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Human perception is not a camera. We do not see the world as it is — we construct it through pattern recognition, attention filtering, and expectation. Understanding these mechanisms is essential for designing interfaces that feel intuitive.
key_concepts: Gestalt principles: proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, figure-ground, common fate
Selective attention: we can only consciously process one thing at a time
Change blindness: we miss changes outside our focus of attention
Pre-attentive processing: color, size, orientation, and motion are detected before conscious attention
Inattentional blindness: we do not see things we are not looking for
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive perception experiments: Gestalt grouping challenges, the invisible gorilla video, and change blindness demonstrations — then discussing design implications of each

review.value: Color, size, orientation, and motion are detected in under 200ms without conscious effort. This is why a red error message on a blue page is seen instantly — use pre-attentive attributes strategically for visual hierarchy.

Users miss changes that occur outside their focus of attention. If an error message appears at the top of the page while the user is focused on a form at the bottom, they may never see it. Place feedback near the user's current focus.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between working memory, long-term memory, and procedural memory in design contexts
Apply cognitive load theory to reduce extraneous load in interfaces
Design for recognition over recall to reduce memory burden
readings: Designing with the Mind in Mind by Jeff Johnson, Ch. 3-5
The Design of Everyday Things by Don Norman, Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Human memory is unreliable, limited, and easily disrupted. Interfaces that depend on users remembering information — codes, navigation paths, previous selections — are fighting against fundamental cognitive limitations.
key_concepts: Working memory: 4 plus or minus 1 chunks, decays in 20-30 seconds without rehearsal
Long-term memory: vast but unreliable — recognition is far easier than recall
Cognitive load theory: intrinsic (task complexity), extraneous (interface friction), germane (learning)
Hick's Law: decision time increases with the number of choices
Progressive disclosure: managing complexity by revealing information gradually
lecture_notes.demonstration: Memory capacity experiment: students attempt to remember sequences of increasing length, then discuss how interface design can work within these limits

review.value: Dropdown menus (recognition: see options, pick one) are easier than text fields (recall: remember and type from memory). Design for recognition by showing options, using autocomplete, and displaying recent items.

Choice reaction time = a + b * log2(n) where n is the number of choices. Reducing options from 20 to 5 dramatically reduces decision time. But oversimplification loses features — progressive disclosure is the balance.

learning_objectives: Identify cognitive biases that affect user decision-making in interfaces
Design ethically with awareness of how biases can be exploited
Apply behavioral economics principles to encourage beneficial user actions
readings: Thinking, Fast and Slow by Daniel Kahneman, Ch. 10-14
Hooked by Nir Eyal, Ch. 1-3 (read critically)

lecture_notes.introduction: Users do not make rational decisions. They are influenced by framing, defaults, anchoring, and social proof. Understanding these biases lets designers either exploit them (dark patterns) or design around them (ethical design).
key_concepts: System 1 (fast, automatic, emotional) vs. System 2 (slow, deliberate, rational) thinking
Default effect: users overwhelmingly accept defaults — design defaults responsibly
Anchoring: the first number users see influences all subsequent judgments
Social proof: users look to others' behavior when uncertain
Loss aversion: the pain of losing is approximately twice the pleasure of gaining
Ethical use of biases: nudging toward beneficial outcomes vs. manipulation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bias identification exercise: analyzing 5 real e-commerce pages for cognitive bias exploitation — identifying which uses are ethical nudges and which are dark patterns

review.value: Approximately 90% of users accept default settings. Ethical design: default to the option that best serves the user (privacy on, reasonable notifications). Unethical: default to the option that maximizes company profit (share data, subscribe to all emails).

'95% success rate' and '5% failure rate' are the same number but feel very different. Interface copy framing affects user confidence and decisions. Choose frames that inform accurately, not that manipulate.

learning_objectives: Apply models of intrinsic and extrinsic motivation to product design
Design for positive emotional experiences: delight, trust, and satisfaction
Understand the psychology of habit formation and its design implications
readings: Emotional Design by Don Norman, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Users are not just information processors — they are emotional beings. Design that accounts for emotion, motivation, and habit formation creates products people want to use, not just products they have to use.
key_concepts: Norman's three levels of emotional design: visceral, behavioral, reflective
Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, and relatedness as motivators
The Hook Model: trigger, action, variable reward, investment
Flow state: the optimal experience between boredom and anxiety
Intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation: rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the motivational design of three apps — Duolingo (gamification), Headspace (habit building), and Strava (social motivation) — identifying which psychological principles each employs

review.value: Three innate psychological needs: Autonomy (feeling in control), Competence (feeling capable), Relatedness (feeling connected). Products that satisfy these needs create intrinsic motivation — users want to use them, not just have to.

Optimal engagement occurs when challenge matches skill level. Too easy = boredom. Too hard = anxiety. Design interfaces that gradually increase complexity as user skill grows — onboarding should scaffold toward mastery.

class_schedule.topic: Learning, Mental Models, and Error Psychology
learning_objectives: Design onboarding that leverages transfer learning from familiar interfaces
Understand the psychology of human error and design error-tolerant systems
Apply spaced repetition and active recall principles to educational UX
readings: Human Error by James Reason, Ch. 1-3
Make It Stick by Brown, Roediger & McDaniel, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Human error is not a personal failure — it is a system design failure. When users make mistakes, the interface design allowed or encouraged it. Understanding error psychology lets us design systems that prevent, detect, and recover from errors gracefully.
key_concepts: Slips (right intention, wrong action) vs. mistakes (wrong intention from wrong mental model)
Swiss cheese model: errors pass through multiple defense layers before causing harm
Transfer learning: users apply knowledge from familiar systems to new ones
Designing for error: constraints, confirmations, undo, and forgiving input
Learning science: spaced repetition, active recall, and interleaving in UX
lecture_notes.demonstration: Error taxonomy exercise: classifying 10 real-world usability errors as slips or mistakes, identifying the design flaw that enabled each, and proposing a prevention mechanism
setup_requirements: Real-world error examples from usability studies

review.value: Slips: the user intended the right action but performed it wrong (tapped the wrong button due to small touch targets). Mistakes: the user performed the intended action but their understanding was wrong (deleted a file thinking it would go to trash, but it was permanently deleted). Each requires a different design solution.

Multiple defense layers (constraints, confirmation, undo, backup) each have holes. An error causes harm only when the holes align across all layers. Design multiple overlapping safeguards, not a single defense.

questions.question: A mobile app shows a success message at the top of the screen after a user submits a form at the bottom of the screen. Most users do not notice the message. Using cognitive psychology principles, explain why this happens and propose a fix.
questions.answer: This is a change blindness / inattentional blindness problem. The user's visual attention is focused on the form submit button at the bottom of the screen. Changes outside the current focus of attention (the success message at the top) are not detected because selective attention limits conscious processing to one spatial area. Fix: Display the success feedback near the user's current focus — immediately below or next to the submit button. Alternatively, use pre-attentive processing attributes (a color change to the button itself, a motion animation radiating from the button) that are detected without requiring focused attention. A third option: animate a checkmark that appears at the submit location and then moves to a persistent status area, guiding the user's attention.

rubric.key: Identifies change blindness or inattentional blindness
Explains selective attention and spatial focus
Proposes fix based on proximity to attention focus
Mentions pre-attentive processing as additional strategy

questions.question: A settings page presents users with 30 options in a single scrollable list. Using cognitive load theory and Hick's Law, explain the problem and redesign the page.
questions.answer: Problems: (1) Hick's Law: 30 options in a flat list creates long decision times. Choice RT = a + b * log2(30), meaning each option adds scanning time. (2) Working memory: users cannot hold 30 options in working memory simultaneously, so they forget earlier options while scanning later ones. (3) Extraneous cognitive load: the undifferentiated list provides no organizational structure to aid processing. Redesign: (1) Group the 30 settings into 5-6 categories (Account, Notifications, Privacy, Display, etc.) — chunking reduces working memory load. (2) Use progressive disclosure — show categories first, expand to show individual settings within each. (3) Add search functionality for users who know what they want. (4) Place the most frequently changed settings at the top (based on analytics data).

rubric.key: Correctly applies Hick's Law to the problem

questions.question: An app uses 'Are you sure you want to miss out on exclusive deals?' as the opt-out text for their marketing email subscription. Analyze this using cognitive bias knowledge and explain why it is ethically problematic.
questions.answer: This is a confirmshaming dark pattern that exploits multiple cognitive biases: (1) Loss aversion — framing the opt-out as 'missing out' triggers the pain of loss (approximately 2x the pleasure of equivalent gain), making users emotionally reluctant to unsubscribe. (2) Scarcity/FOMO — 'exclusive deals' implies scarce opportunities, triggering fear of missing out. (3) Social pressure — the framing implies opting out is an irrational choice ('who would want to miss deals?'). Ethically problematic because: it uses emotional manipulation to override informed rational choice. The user's decision to unsubscribe is valid and should be respected without guilt. Ethical alternative: 'Unsubscribe from marketing emails' — clear, neutral, respectful of user autonomy.

rubric.key: Explains why it is ethically problematic

questions.question: Classify the following as a slip or a mistake, and explain the design solution for each: (a) A user accidentally deletes an email while trying to archive it. (b) A user sets their alarm for 7PM instead of 7AM.
questions.answer: (a) Slip — the user intended to archive but performed the wrong action, likely because the delete and archive buttons are adjacent and similar-looking. Design solution: increase the physical distance between destructive (delete) and common (archive) actions, use distinct visual treatments (red for delete, neutral for archive), and provide immediate undo for all destructive actions. (b) Mistake — the user intended to set 7PM (believed they selected the right option) but their mental model was wrong. Design solution: make AM/PM highly prominent in the time picker with clear visual distinction (sun icon for AM, moon for PM), show a confirmation with explicit AM/PM ('Alarm set for 7:00 AM — tomorrow morning'), and use contextual warnings ('7:00 PM is in 11 hours — did you mean 7:00 AM?').

rubric.key: Correctly classifies (a) as slip with reasoning
Correctly classifies (b) as mistake with reasoning

major_assignments.title: Cognitive Psychology Interface Audit
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive cognitive psychology audit of a digital product. Evaluate how the interface aligns with or violates principles of perception, memory, decision-making, and error psychology. Propose redesigns backed by cognitive science evidence.
objectives: Apply cognitive psychology principles systematically to evaluate a real product
Identify design patterns that align with human cognition
Identify design patterns that work against human cognition
Propose evidence-based redesigns grounded in cognitive science
deliverables: Cognitive audit report (10-15 pages) organized by psychology principle
Annotated screenshots documenting each finding with cognitive science explanation
Redesigned screens (3-5 key screens) with before/after comparison
Cognitive science bibliography supporting each finding and recommendation
Presentation of top findings to the class

criteria: All major cognitive psychology areas covered: perception, memory, attention, decision-making, error
Both positive patterns (aligned with cognition) and negative patterns (violating cognition) identified
Findings are specific with exact locations and descriptions
Analysis goes beyond surface to underlying cognitive mechanisms
Each finding cites specific cognitive principles or research
Explanations accurately represent the underlying psychology
Bibliography includes primary sources, not just design articles
Connections between psychology and design are clear and valid
Redesigns directly address identified cognitive issues
Each redesign is justified with specific psychology principles
Before/after comparison shows clear improvement
Redesigns are practical and implementable
Top findings clearly communicated with visual evidence
Cognitive science explanations are accessible to a design audience
Recommendations are prioritized by user impact
Presentation demonstrates deep understanding of course material
Thorough cognitive audit covering all major psychology areas
Findings grounded in specific cognitive science research
High-quality redesigns with evidence-based justification
Clear and engaging presentation of findings

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on cognitive psychology principles and their design applications

psychology_labs.description: Weekly labs applying specific psychology principles to interface analysis and redesign

cognitive_audit_project.description: Comprehensive cognitive psychology audit of a real product with redesigns

research_paper_reviews.description: Critical reviews of cognitive psychology research papers with design implications

final_exam.description: Comprehensive exam on cognitive psychology principles and their design applications

course_policies.attendance: Perception experiments and interactive demonstrations require in-person participation. More than 3 absences affect participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Lab exercises due weekly. Late submissions penalized 10% per day. The cognitive audit has a firm deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of psychology concepts is encouraged. All audit work and redesigns must be individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Cite all psychology research sources. Accurately represent research findings — do not oversimplify or misrepresent cognitive science to support your design arguments.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to digital products for analysis, Figma for redesign work, academic database access for psychology research papers, and presentation software.
